title: Electrical Spectroscopy of Intervalley Relaxation in WSe$_2$ Transistors
abstract: We show that the transconductance of multilayer WSe$_2$ field-effect transistors serves as a direct electrical spectrometer of the intervalley relaxation time $τ_{\rm iv}$, previously accessible only by ultrafast optical techniques. Extending an equilibrium valley-thermodynamics framework with a single relaxation equation for the $Γ$-valley carrier fraction $f_Γ(t)$, we predict three signatures: (i)~a Lorentzian transconductance $g_m(ω)=g_{m,0}+g_{m,v}^0/(1+iωτ_{\rm iv})$, whose imaginary part peaks at $ω_c=τ_{\rm iv}^{-1}$ with opposite signs for bilayer and trilayer; (ii)~a two-stage current transient after a gate step, exhibiting bilayer overshoot or trilayer undershoot; and (iii)~sweep-rate-proportional hysteresis whose gate-voltage profile and layer-number sign reversal distinguish valley from trap-induced dynamics. All three signatures provide quantitative electrical access to $τ_{\rm iv}$ with standard rf and dc instrumentation.
summary: This work develops a novel electrical spectroscopy technique to measure the intervalley relaxation time ($\tau_{iv}$) in WSe$_2$ transistors. By analyzing three complementary signatures—AC transconductance, current transients, and hysteresis—the authors provide quantitative access to $\tau_{iv}$ using standard electronic measurements rather than complex ultrafast optics.
main_problem: To establish a direct electrical spectroscopic method for measuring the intervalley relaxation time ($\tau_{iv}$) in WSe$_2$ transistors, bypassing reliance on ultrafast optical techniques.
main_result: Three distinct electrical signatures—frequency-dependent transconductance, current transients, and sweep-rate hysteresis—were shown to provide quantitative access to $\tau_{iv}$.
method: The analysis extends an equilibrium valley-thermodynamics framework by modeling the time evolution of the $\Gamma$-valley carrier fraction using a single relaxation equation.
model_or_system: The system is multilayer WSe$_2$ field-effect transistors, where the valley population acts as a dynamic internal state variable governed by the relaxation rate $\tau_{iv}$.
key_observables: Frequency-dependent transconductance $g_m(\omega)$, two-stage current transients (overshoot/undershoot), and sweep-rate-proportional hysteresis.
important_parameters: Intervalley relaxation time ($\tau_{iv}$); characteristic frequency $\omega_c = 1/\tau_{iv}$; temperature dependence following a Bose–Einstein form.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on extending the equilibrium valley-thermodynamics framework and using first-order approximations for slow gate sweeps to derive the hysteresis current.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the schematic measurement setup, the layer-dependent overshoot/undershoot in transients, Lorentzian peaks in $g_m(\omega)$ at $\omega_c$, and linear scaling of hysteresis current with sweep rate $|r|$.
paper_structure: The paper develops a theoretical framework based on a relaxation equation for valley population, predicts three measurable electrical signatures (AC spectroscopy, DC transients, hysteresis), and demonstrates how these signatures allow quantitative extraction of $\tau_{iv}$ using standard RF/DC equipment.
why_interesting: The use of dynamic response functions ($g_m(\omega)$) to probe relaxation rates ($\tau_{iv}$) is a classic problem in open quantum systems, linking transport measurements directly to the system's coherence or decay timescales.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Overview of transconductance spectroscopy of intervalley relaxation. (a) Schematic of a multilayer WSe2 transistor in dual-gate geometry with hBN dielectric [32, 33]. An AC gate voltage VGS(t) = V0 + δV eiωt drives the device; the drain current ID(ω) is detected by a lock-in amplifier to extract gm(ω) = dID(ω)/dVGS(ω). (b) Valley population fΓ(t) in the Γ valley (solid) compared to the instantaneous equilibrium value (dashed), illustrating the phase delay ∆t = ϕ/ω arising from the finite intervalley relaxation time τiv. (c) Predicted imaginary part of the valley transconductance Im[gm(ω)] for bilayer (blue) and trilayer (red) WSe2. Opposite-sign Lorentzian peaks centered at ωc = τ...

Figure 2 illustrates the physical picture: a gate-voltage step (VGS : V1 →V2) causes the charge to jump instan- taneously while fΓ exponentially relaxes toward the new equilibrium, producing a two-stage current response. Table I lists the numerical parameters used throughout this work. The effective masses and K–Γ splittings are taken from first-principles calculations [11, 13], and the mobilities are representative experimental values [6, 7]. The intervalley relaxation time τiv enters only as a free parameter; no specific numerical value is assumed in any figure.

FIG. 2. Two-stage current response to a gate step ∆VGS = 0.2 V (upper panel) for bilayer (blue) and trilayer (red) WSe2 (lower panel, thick lines). Thin dashed lines (fΓ = fi, frozen) show the valley-frozen reference: charge responds instanta- neously (τRC ≪τiv) while fΓ remains at its pre-step value fi. Valley relaxation then drives the current toward ID(∞): over- shoot for the bilayer and undershoot for the trilayer, with a characteristic timescale τiv. The opposite-sign departures re- flect sgn(g0 m,v) reversing between layers.

FIG. 3. Frequency-dependent transconductance for bilayer (solid, blue) and trilayer (dashed, red) WSe2 at T = 300 K, evaluated at the gate voltage where |g0 m,v| is maximum. Shaded regions mark three dynamical regimes: adiabatic (ωτiv ≪1), spectroscopy window (ωτiv ∼1, yellow), and valley-frozen (ωτiv ≫1). (a) Real part Re[gm(ω)]/g(0) m nor- malized to the dc value; dotted lines are the high-frequency limits gm,0/g(0) m . (b) Imaginary part Im[gm(ω)]/|g0 m,v|; the extremum at ωc = τ −1 iv (vertical dotted) has opposite sign for bilayer (g0 m,v < 0) and trilayer (g0 m,v > 0).

FIG. 4. Low-frequency fingerprints of delayed intervalley relaxation. (a) Signed transient amplitude ∆Itr D/ID(∞) vs gate- step size ∆VGS at T = 300 K, evaluated at the gate voltage of maximum |χv|. Bilayer (blue) overshoots and trilayer (red) undershoots, encoding sgn(g0 m,v); amplitudes grow linearly then saturate. The dotted line marks the ∆VGS = 0.2 V step of Fig. 2. (b) Hysteresis current ∆Ihys D (VGS) [Eq. (12)] for bilayer (solid, blue family) and trilayer (dashed, red family) at normalized sweep rates ˜r = rτiv = 0.05, 0.15, 0.30 (labeled).
